posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked: I’m in the market for a new decanter and would love to hear about your favorites. Ideally something easy to clean (can be put into the dishwasher), a slightly wider bottom, and extremely elegant looking (I personally would prefer something modern or mid-century modern). No Pyrex beakers or lead allowed!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Shan_A:

I’m in the market for a new decanter and would love to hear about your favorites. Ideally something easy to clean (can be put into the dishwasher), a slightly wider bottom, and extremely elegant looking (I personally would prefer something modern or mid-century modern). No Pyrex beakers or lead allowed!

Zalto’s decanter is nice.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
It’s not fancy, but I like the Riedel Cabernet decanter. They used to have a cheaper version sold at Target, I bought several when they were on sale for $20 or less. I think glass, not crystal.

-Al

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.cooked:
I love the decanters from La Soufflerie. Some are rather small and some are in a good size.
In the end they just have to be a different vessel to pour into and nothing else, so they might as well just look good.

posts.name: Terry H a r r i s
posts.cooked:
The decanter I use all the time, when opening only one bottle, is a very basic glass decanter.

For multiple bottles, however, I’ve got a number of other nice crystal decanters. Here’s one I like that I picked up from Williams Sonoma:

img198o710×710 31.2 KB

https://www.williams-sonoma.com/products/williams-sonoma-estate-decanter/?cm_src=SocialShareLink

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I use the Zalto carafes a lot, which have the advantage of being easy to fit in a refrigerator or ice bucket if you want to cool down whatever you’re serving…

Also love English Georgian cut glass, and finally found a magnum decanter in great shape after a few years looking for the right one Amusingly, it cost a touch less than Riedel’s ridiculous “Swan”.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.cooked: Oh yes, the “fit into an ice-bucket” thing is real!

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Al_Osterheld:

It’s not fancy, but I like the Riedel Cabernet decanter. They used to have a cheaper version sold at Target, I bought several when they were on sale for $20 or less. I think glass, not crystal.

-Al

Same here. I have 12 of them. I like the small base, as they fit neatly in the cabinet and, as in @William_Kelley 's example, in the fridge or ice bucket. I have several others, including the “ridiculous swan,” which I got as a gift, but rarely reach for them.

Once, Jonathan’s brother was visiting and looked into the glass cabinet. “What do you do with 12 decanters?” Ummm…same as with 1, only 12 times.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I collect old decanters, many of them probably have some, or a lot of lead. Having done a fair amount of research, I am super careful, although apparently it takes several weeks for any lead to leach into the wine.

When I do use them and if a long decant is need, I do it in another decanter, and then pour it in just before serving. There is something special about having wine from the same decanter as people had a couple of centuries ago, and speculate who they were and what they talked about.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked:
I can be lazy. 12 decanters means I could let several just sit there after use until I get around to washing them.

For the Zalto carafes, do folks prefer using the 75 for 750ml, or oversizing it to the 150?

posts.name: Justin Lavine
posts.cooked:
Recently purchased a Glasvin decanter and I’m a big fan.

It’s nothing fancy but definitely an upgrade from the Amazon or Target decanter I had previously been using.

posts.name: Josh Grossman
posts.cooked: I’m even worse than lead. I collect uranium glass. The green decanter is uranium glass and I have a couple gorgeous sets of Champagne glasses that are uranium.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
I like my Zalto.

But for tastings I bought a bunch of IKEA decanters (STORSNIT). Cheap and works just fine. Goes into the dishwasher without any problem.

1000006255616×886 56 KB

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked:
Due to my oafish handling my favorite decanters are no longer with us. I make do with these remaining two. A simple cheap glass one from Crate & Barrel in the late 1970’s, and an older hand-me-down.

IMG_42343264×2448 2.36 MB

I do like the looks of that Reidel Cabernet decanter that was mentioned and repeatedly recommended.

posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.cooked:
For most bottles, my go-to is the Riedel Syrah decanter. It’s similar to the standard Riedel decanter but features a spout that prevents drips. While it’s been discontinued, you can still find them on eBay for around $35.

IMG_83671920×2560 289 KB

For larger formats, I swear by my 2000 cc Erlenmeyer flask. It’s under $40, works like a charm, and never fails to spark entertaining conversations.

IMG_83681920×2560 294 KB

Both are dishwasher safe.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
Actually, the first decanter I used was from a cheap Provencal wine I bought many years ago that came in a decanter shaped bottle. I bought it mainly for the bottle. Looked roughly like this although flatter on the bottom.

Screen Shot 2025-01-19 at 9.23.59 AM940×874 109 KB

Wasn’t very high quality glass, though, and eventually became difficult to keep it looking clean.

-Al
